planting.note: Plant fresh rhizomes in late summer or early fall at 2–3 in depth in moist, humus-rich woodland soil; from seed, expect 4–7 years to first bloom as seeds require double dormancy — a warm period followed by cold before germination, then another year before the seedling emerges. Plant divisions are far more practical.

directions: Plant in consistently moist, deep, humus-rich, slightly acidic woodland soil in full to partial shade; nodding trillium is distinguished by its white flower that hangs below the leaves on a recurved peduncle, making it easy to overlook in the wild but charming on close inspection. The flower faces downward and is pollinated by bumblebees that must hover or enter from beneath. Plant rhizomes pointed-end up at a depth of 2–3 inches in fall, and mulch with shredded leaves to mimic the natural leaf litter of its woodland floor habitat. Never harvest plants from the wild — all trilliums are extremely slow-growing from seed and wild populations decline quickly when disturbed. Shade and cool, moist conditions are essential throughout the growing season.

planting.note: Direct sow fresh seed in fall for spring germination after natural cold stratification; or cold-stratify seeds for 60 days in the refrigerator and sow indoors in late winter. Plants also spread slowly by rhizomes and can be divided in spring.

directions: Plant in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic soil in partial to full shade under deciduous trees; early meadow rue is one of the first woodland wildflowers to bloom in spring, with delicate, dangling male and female flowers on separate plants (it is dioecious) appearing before the trees leaf out. The blue-green, columbine-like foliage is attractive throughout summer and provides a fine-textured contrast against bold-leaved hostas and ferns. Wind pollination is the norm; if growing for seed set, plant several individuals to ensure both sexes are present. The plant goes partially dormant in hot, dry summers — keep soil moist and mulch well. It naturalizes quietly beneath oaks, maples, and beeches.

planting.note: Plant divisions or container plants in spring in consistently moist, humus-rich, acidic soil; log fern resents drought and requires reliably moist conditions throughout the growing season.

directions: Plant in consistently moist to wet, humus-rich, acidic soil in partial to full shade in zones 5–9; log fern is a naturally occurring hybrid between the Goldie's fern (Dryopteris goldiana) and the swamp wood fern (Dryopteris ludoviciana), combining the large size of Goldie's fern with the semi-evergreen habit of the swamp fern. The broad, lustrous dark green fronds are among the most striking of any eastern US fern, and the plant is especially valuable in the Southeast because it thrives in the heat and humidity of zones 7–9 where many northern ferns languish. It is commonly found growing on the moist, decaying logs and hummocks of southeastern bottomland forests, giving rise to its common name. Mulch heavily in zones 5–6 to protect the crowns in winter.

planting.note: Plant hardwood cuttings or stakes directly into moist, sandy, or silty streambank soil in very early spring; stems 12–24 in long can be pushed directly into moist soil where they root almost instantly, making sandbar willow the easiest willow to propagate for streambank restoration.

directions: Plant in wet to moist, sandy or silty soil in full sun along stream banks, pond margins, and flood-prone areas; sandbar willow colonizes bare mineral soils and gravel bars faster than almost any other native woody plant, making it invaluable for erosion control and streambank stabilization following disturbance. The long, narrow silver-green leaves give the plant an elegant, fine-textured appearance that contrasts nicely with coarser riparian vegetation. Mature plants form dense thickets through aggressive root sprouting, providing nesting cover for warblers, wrens, and yellowthroats. The species is ecologically keystone in southwestern riparian corridors where it provides critical streambank structure and willow-dependent pollinator forage.

planting.note: Direct sow acorns in fall immediately after they fall from the tree, pressing them 1 inch deep in a prepared bed or container of well-drained soil; acorns don't need stratification and germinate in spring. For transplanting, move only small seedlings — oaks develop a deep taproot quickly and large transplants fail.

directions: Plant in moist to wet, acidic soil in full sun; pin oak is the most widely planted native oak in urban landscapes because of its symmetrical pyramidal form, fast growth (1–2 ft/year when young), ease of transplanting, and tolerance of clay and periodic flooding. The deeply lobed leaves turn brilliant red in fall, and the tree retains many of its dead lower branches in a distinctive downswept arrangement — a characteristic silhouette. Pin oak strictly requires acidic soil (pH 5.0–6.5); in high-pH soils the tree develops iron chlorosis with yellowing leaves and declining vigor. Avoid high-pH soils or amend with elemental sulfur before planting. The small acorns are among the most important wildlife foods in eastern North America, consumed by over 100 bird and mammal species.
